Interactive Lab

Signal Generator + FFT Spectrum Analyzer

Signal Generator

50Hz
1.00
120Hz
0.50
0.10

Time-Domain Waveform

FFT Magnitude Spectrum

Technical Details:
  • Sampling rate: 1024 Hz
  • Number of samples: 1024 points
  • Frequency resolution: Δf = fs/N = 1024/1024 = 1 Hz
  • Maximum analyzable frequency: fs/2 = 512 Hz (Nyquist)
  • Signal model: x[n] = A1sin(2πf1n/fs) + A2sin(2πf2n/fs) + noise

🧪 Guided Experiments

Experiment 1: Spectrum of a Single Sine Wave

Settings: f1=100Hz, A1=1.0, turn off f2 (A2=0), noise=0, window=Hann

Expected result: The time domain shows a clean sine wave. The spectrum has a sharp peak at 100Hz with almost no leakage to adjacent bins.

Try this: Switch to the Rectangular window and observe the sidelobes (leakage) appearing around 100Hz.

Experiment 2: Two Closely Spaced Frequencies

Settings: f1=100Hz, f2=108Hz, A1=A2=1.0, noise=0

Expected result: With the Hann window, you should see two separate peaks. Switch to the Rectangular window, and the two peaks may merge (because the wider main lobe causes mutual interference).

Try this: Move f2 closer to 104Hz and observe when the two peaks become indistinguishable.

Experiment 3: Weak Signal in Noise

Settings: f1=100Hz A1=1.0, f2=200Hz A2=0.1, noise=0.5

Expected result: The 100Hz peak is clearly visible, but the 200Hz peak may be buried in noise. Switch to the Blackman window (low sidelobes), and the 200Hz peak should become easier to identify.

Experiment 4: Harmonic Structure of a Square Wave

Settings: (requires changing the waveform to square in the code) f1=50Hz square wave, A1=1.0

Expected result: The spectrum shows peaks at 50, 150, 250, 350... Hz (odd harmonics), with amplitudes decaying as 1/n. This is a practical verification of the Fourier series.

Comprehensive Quiz: 20 Questions on Fourier Analysis

Covers core concepts from all six parts. Each question tests understanding, not formula memorization.

Question 1: In the Hilbert space L2[0, 2π], why can {ejnω} serve as a basis?




Explanation: A basis in a Hilbert space requires two conditions: (1) Orthogonality -- $\langle e^{jm\omega}, e^{jn\omega}\rangle = 2\pi\delta_{mn}$; (2) Completeness -- no nonzero function is orthogonal to all basis elements. {ejnω} satisfies both conditions, thus forming an orthogonal basis. Having unit amplitude is merely normalization, not the fundamental reason. There are many types of orthogonal function sets (Legendre, Hermite...), so they are not unique.

Question 2: The Dirac delta function δ(t) is not a function in the traditional sense. What mathematical framework does it belong to?




Explanation: The Theory of Distributions, established by Laurent Schwartz in the 1940s, gave δ(t) a rigorous mathematical definition. δ is a linear functional acting on test functions: $\langle \delta, \varphi \rangle = \varphi(0)$. It is not an ordinary function that is "infinite at the origin."

Question 3: What does the Uncertainty Principle tell us?




Explanation: The Gabor-Heisenberg uncertainty principle: $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$. The more concentrated a signal is in time (short pulse), the more spread out its spectrum becomes (wide bandwidth), and vice versa. This is a mathematical consequence of the Fourier transform, not a technical limitation. A Gaussian signal achieves equality (minimum uncertainty).

Question 4: What is the fundamental difference between Fourier Series (FS) and the Fourier Transform (FT)?




Explanation: The spectrum of a periodic signal is discrete (nonzero only at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency), represented by Fourier series (summation). The spectrum of an aperiodic signal is continuous, represented by the Fourier transform (integration). FS is not an approximation of FT -- they are different mathematical tools for different types of signals. Within the framework of distribution theory, FS can be viewed as a special case of FT (the FT of a periodic signal is a series of delta functions).

Question 5: The sampling theorem requires fs > 2fmax. If a signal has a bandwidth of 100-200 Hz (bandpass signal), what is the minimum sampling rate?




Explanation: The Bandpass Sampling Theorem states that a bandpass signal only requires fs ≥ 2B (B = bandwidth), not 2fmax. Here B = 200-100 = 100Hz, so fs ≥ 200Hz is sufficient. However, fs must be carefully chosen so that the down-converted spectrum does not overlap. For example, fs = 250Hz works, but fs = 300Hz may cause aliasing.

Question 6: DFT length N = 1024, sampling rate fs = 10 kHz. What is the frequency resolution? If you need to resolve two frequencies 1Hz apart, what is the minimum N?




Explanation: Δf = fs/N = 10000/1024 = 9.77 Hz. To achieve 1Hz resolution: N = fs/Δf = 10000/1 = 10000. Note: Zero-padding can make the spectrum appear smoother (interpolation), but does not truly improve resolution -- resolution depends only on the actual data length.

Question 7: Regarding the relationship between the Z-transform and DTFT, which of the following is correct?




Explanation: The Z-transform $X(z) = \sum x[n]z^{-n}$ is defined over the entire z-plane (converging within the ROC). Setting z = ejω (unit circle) yields the DTFT. However, the prerequisite is that the ROC must include the unit circle; otherwise, the DTFT does not exist (e.g., unstable systems). The Z-transform is more general than the DTFT because it can analyze unstable systems.

Question 8: You perform FFT analysis on a single-frequency signal using a rectangular window. If the signal frequency falls exactly between two FFT bins (non-integer bin), what happens?




Explanation: This is the classic spectral leakage problem. A finite-length FFT essentially multiplies the signal by a rectangular window. In the frequency domain, this equals the convolution of the signal spectrum with a sinc function. The sinc sidelobes (-13dB) "leak" energy to bins far from the true frequency. Using a Hann window (-31.5dB sidelobes) or Blackman window (-58dB) significantly improves this.

Question 9: What is the main advantage of the Welch method over directly computing the periodogram? What is the trade-off?




Explanation: The Welch method divides long data into short segments, applies a window and FFT to each segment, then averages the results. Averaging K segments reduces the variance by a factor of 1/K. However, shorter segments mean reduced frequency resolution (larger Δf). This is a trade-off between frequency resolution and statistical stability -- not a technical limitation, but an inevitable consequence of the uncertainty principle.

Question 10: The MUSIC algorithm can achieve higher frequency resolution than FFT. Why?




Explanation: MUSIC is a parametric method -- it assumes the signal consists of p sinusoids plus noise. Using eigendecomposition of the correlation matrix, it separates the signal subspace from the noise subspace, then searches for steering vectors orthogonal to the noise subspace to locate frequencies. Because it leverages the additional information from model assumptions, it can break through the Fourier resolution limit (Δf = 1/T). The trade-off: the number of signals p must be correctly estimated; incorrect model assumptions lead to poor results.

Question 11: What is the primary purpose of the Hilbert transform?




Explanation: The Hilbert transform converts a real signal x(t) into an analytic signal z(t) = x(t) + jH{x(t)}, where H{} is the Hilbert transform. The magnitude of the analytic signal |z(t)| = instantaneous amplitude (envelope), and the derivative of its phase arg(z(t)) = instantaneous frequency. This is extremely important in vibration analysis envelope spectra, AM demodulation in communications, speech analysis, and other fields.

Question 12: In cepstrum analysis, what does "liftering" mean?




Explanation: The name "cepstrum" is formed by reversing the first four letters of "spectrum." Similarly, "liftering" is an anagram of "filtering." In the quefrency domain, low-quefrency components correspond to the overall spectral shape (e.g., formant envelope), while high-quefrency components correspond to fine spectral structure (e.g., harmonic spacing). Liftering means truncating or windowing in the quefrency domain to select specific cepstral components.

Question 13: You need to analyze a 100ms chirp signal (frequency sweeping linearly from 1kHz to 5kHz). What should the STFT window length be?




Explanation: The STFT window length is a trade-off between time resolution and frequency resolution. The chirp sweeps 4kHz over 100ms, at a rate of 40kHz/s. To track frequency changes, the window must be much shorter than the chirp length. A 5-10ms window: time resolution of 5-10ms (sufficient to observe frequency changes), frequency resolution of 100-200Hz (acceptable). A 1ms window is too short: frequency resolution of 1000Hz, making it impossible to discern the frequency clearly. A 100ms window is equivalent to not performing STFT at all.

Question 14: What is the greatest advantage of the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) over STFT?




Explanation: STFT uses a fixed window length, so the time-frequency resolution is the same for all frequencies. CWT uses a scalable wavelet: when analyzing low frequencies, the wavelet is automatically stretched (good frequency resolution, poor time resolution); when analyzing high frequencies, the wavelet is automatically compressed (good time resolution, poor frequency resolution). This "multi-resolution" property is ideal for analyzing signals that contain both slow low-frequency variations and high-frequency transients.

Question 15: In an OFDM system, what happens if the CP length is shorter than the channel delay spread?




Explanation: The CP serves two functions: (1) absorbing the residual from the previous symbol (preventing ISI); (2) making channel convolution equivalent to circular convolution (enabling pointwise multiplication in the FFT domain). If the CP is too short, both functions fail, resulting in both ISI (inter-symbol interference) and ICI (inter-carrier interference). All subcarriers are affected, not just the edge ones. This is one of the most critical parameters in OFDM system design.

Question 16: In FMCW radar, what does increasing the chirp bandwidth B improve?




Explanation: Range resolution ΔR = c/(2B). B = 1GHz yields ΔR = 15cm. B = 4GHz yields ΔR = 3.75cm. Velocity resolution is determined by the coherent processing interval T_CPI (Δv = λ/(2T_CPI)) and is independent of B. Maximum range depends on power and chirp duration. Angular resolution depends on the antenna array.

Question 17: An engineer observes the 1st, 2nd, 3rd, and 4th harmonics of BPFO clearly in the vibration envelope spectrum. What does this indicate?




Explanation: BPFO = Ball Pass Frequency, Outer race. The appearance of BPFO and its harmonics in the envelope spectrum indicates a localized defect on the outer race (such as pitting). Each time a rolling element passes the defect point, it produces an impact at the BPFO frequency. The presence of harmonics indicates that the impact waveform is not a perfect sinusoid but contains harmonic components. If the inner race were defective, BPFI would be observed instead, typically modulated by the shaft rotation frequency (1X) because the inner race rotates with the shaft and the defect moves in and out of the load zone.

Question 18: In array signal processing, what problem arises when the antenna spacing d > λ/2? What is the time-domain analogy?




Explanation: The spatial Nyquist theorem requires d ≤ λ/2. Antenna spacing is the spatial sampling interval, and λ/2 is the spatial Nyquist interval. When d > λ/2, plane waves from different directions produce the same phase difference across the antenna array (just as different frequencies produce the same discrete sequence after sampling in the time domain), resulting in grating lobes = spatial aliasing. The beamformer cannot distinguish between a real target and a false target in the grating lobe direction.

Question 19: Regarding HRV frequency-domain analysis, which of the following is a common misconception?




Explanation: This is the most widespread misconception in HRV analysis. The LF (0.04-0.15Hz) band is modulated by both sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems -- the baroreflex mechanism keeps both active in this frequency band. The 1996 Task Force report explicitly stated that LF cannot simply be equated with sympathetic activity. Only HF (0.15-0.4Hz) relatively purely reflects parasympathetic activity (respiratory sinus arrhythmia). The LF/HF ratio is at best a rough estimate of sympatho-vagal "balance."

Question 20: In OLA (Overlap-Add) fast convolution, what condition must the FFT length N satisfy? What happens if it is not satisfied?




Explanation: OLA/OLS computes circular convolution using FFT. The linear convolution of two sequences of length L and M has length L+M-1. If the FFT length N < L+M-1, the circular convolution result "wraps around," causing the tail and head to overlap -- this is time-domain aliasing. N ≥ L+M-1 ensures circular convolution equals linear convolution. Additionally, choosing N as a power of 2 is for efficiency (Radix-2 FFT), not a correctness requirement.

About This Platform

This educational platform is a comprehensive, graduate-level online resource on Fourier Analysis, covering six major parts from mathematical foundations to engineering practice.

Course Structure

PartTopicSections
Part IMathematical Foundations4 sections
Part IIFour Core Fourier Transforms + Z-Transform6 sections
Part IIISpectral Estimation4 sections
Part IVAnalytic Signals & Cepstrum3 sections
Part VTime-Frequency Analysis5 sections
Part VIEngineering Practice10 sections

Design Philosophy

  • Intuition First: Each concept is first explained in plain language ("why"), then formalized with equations, and finally accompanied by rigorous derivations in expandable <details> blocks.
  • Problem-Driven: Starting from real-world questions -- "Why does the FFT on an FPGA lack precision?" "Why does the OFDM CP need to be so long?" -- rather than from abstract definitions.
  • Engineering Connection: Every theoretical concept is paired with concrete industrial application examples and real numerical parameters.
  • Interactive Exploration: Built-in interactive charts and a lab environment allow learners to adjust parameters and observe results firsthand.

Target Audience

  • Graduate students in Electrical Engineering / Electronics / Communications / Computer Science
  • Signal Processing / DSP Engineers
  • Vibration Analysis / Predictive Maintenance Engineers
  • Biomedical Engineering / Neuroscience Researchers
  • Radar / Communication System Design Engineers

Technical Information

  • Language: English. Technical terms include original terminology where appropriate.
  • Math Typesetting: Mathematical formulas are marked with the CSS class fm, supporting MathJax/KaTeX rendering.
  • Interactive Charts: Div containers with the plot class, rendered by JavaScript charting libraries (e.g., Plotly.js, Chart.js).

Key References

  1. Oppenheim, A.V. & Schafer, R.W. Discrete-Time Signal Processing, 3rd Ed., Pearson, 2010.
  2. Haykin, S. & Van Veen, B. Signals and Systems, 2nd Ed., Wiley, 2003.
  3. Proakis, J.G. & Manolakis, D.G. Digital Signal Processing, 4th Ed., Pearson, 2007.
  4. Randall, R.B. Vibration-based Condition Monitoring, Wiley, 2011.
  5. Goldsmith, A. Wireless Communications, Cambridge University Press, 2005.
  6. Richards, M.A. Fundamentals of Radar Signal Processing, 2nd Ed., McGraw-Hill, 2014.
  7. Van Trees, H.L. Optimum Array Processing, Wiley, 2002.
  8. Mallat, S. A Wavelet Tour of Signal Processing, 3rd Ed., Academic Press, 2009.
  9. Task Force of ESC/NASPE. "Heart rate variability: Standards of measurement," Circulation, 93(5), 1996.
  10. 3GPP TS 38.211, "NR; Physical channels and modulation," Release 17.
